TEXT.citation: Early, R. J., & Whitehorn, J. (2003). One hundred Paiwan texts. Pacific Linguistics, Research School of Pacific and Asian Studies, The Australian National University.
TEXT.BibTeX_citation: @book{100paiwantexts, author = {Robert Early and John Whitehorn}, title = {One Hundred Paiwan Texts}, year = {2003}, publisher = {Pacific Linguistics, Research School of Pacific and Asian Studies, The Australian National University}}

S.FORM: ka sitsuayan ka mairaw a kadjunangan, saka zaɫum anga pulingelingetj a gadu, manu izua namalauɫ a keḍikeḍi a gadu.
ka sicuayan ka mairaw a kadjunangan, saka zaljum anga pulingelingetj a gadu, manu izua namalaulj a kedrikedri a gadu.
S.TRANSL: Once upon a time the earth dissolved, and the mountains everywhere became water. There was just one small mountain left.

S.FORM: qau matsay a mapuɫat azua tsautsau.
qau macay a mapuljat azua caucau.
sa izua maḍusa a nasekelay tua ritsing nua ɫavilu itjua gadu, aya.
sa izua madrusa a nasekelay tua ricing nua ljavilu itjua gadu, aya.
S.TRANSL: There were just two people left who hung onto a trichodesma tree’s branches on the mountain, they say.

S.FORM: qau makuɫi a zaɫum.
qau makulji a zaljum.
qau mevaɫut azua nasekelay a marekaka.
qau mevaljut azua nasekelay a marekaka.
S.TRANSL: and the siblings who had hung onto the tree survived.

S.FORM: nekanga nu sapuy; nekanga anemanema.
nekanga nu sapuy; nekanga anemanema.
S.TRANSL: There was no fire; there was nothing.

S.FORM: qau tsikemen anga azua rugus nua ɫavilu a sinikelayan, sa patuti.
qau cikemen anga azua rugus nua ljavilu a sinikelayan, sa patuti.
S.TRANSL: So they snapped off twigs of the trichodesma tree to which they had hung, and used them as drills.

S.FORM: qau maqati anga a sapuy.
qau maqati anga a sapuy.
qau petsungu anga; izuanga sikesa.
qau pecungu anga; izuanga sikesa.
S.TRANSL: and they could light a fire; they had the means to cook.

S.FORM: sa azua maḍusanga tiamadju; qau marepuvaɫaw.
sa azua madrusanga tiamadju; qau marepuvaljaw.
S.TRANSL: Those two who were left married one another.

S.FORM: manu puaɫak tu marka mavutsa, tu marka mapiɫay, tu matjubang, aya.
manu pualjak tu marka mavuca, tu marka mapiljay, tu matjubang, aya.
S.TRANSL: And they had children who were blind, crippled or scrofulous, they say.

S.FORM: saka nu matjubang pasa pairangen; nu mapiɫay nu mavutsa pasa paqaluqaluin; nu nanguanguaq a aɫak niamadju, aya tjautsikel.
saka nu matjubang pasa pairangen; nu mapiljay nu mavuca pasa paqaluqaluin; nu nanguanguaq a aljak niamadju, aya tjaucikel.
S.TRANSL: The scrofulous ones were sent off to the plains people; the crippled and blind were sent to the east Paiwans; the healthy ones they took as their own children, according to the story.

S.FORM: sa azua marekaka tiamadju; mavan nu sika maya a maqati a mavutsa matjubang mapiɫay, aya.
sa azua marekaka tiamadju; mavan nu sika maya a maqati a mavuca matjubang mapiljay, aya.
S.TRANSL: Now those two were siblings; and that’s why the children were born blind, scrofulous or crippled like that, they say.

S.FORM: sa ka tsuatsuay anga marepuvaɫavaɫaw, djemaɫun anga tua marka nanguaq.
sa ka cuacuay anga marepuvaljavaljaw, djemaljun anga tua marka nanguaq.
S.TRANSL: After a long time of intermarriage they got to having healthy children;

S.FORM: maqati anga a tsautsau a inika mavutsa, a inika mapiɫay, a inika matjubang, aya a tjautsikel a masi tsuay.
maqati anga a caucau a inika mavuca, a inika mapiljay, a inika matjubang, aya a tjaucikel a masi cuay.
S.TRANSL: and so there were people who were not blind or crippled or scrofulous, according to the old story.
